title.zh: 卷七十一 雋疏于薛平彭傳
title.en: Volume 71: Jun, Shu, Yu, Xue, Ping and Peng
title.raw: 卷七十一 雋疏于薛平彭傳 Volume 71: Jun, Shu, Yu, Xue, Ping and Peng

sentences.zh: 雋不疑

translations.literal: Jun Buyi.
translations.idiomatic: Jun Buyi

sentences.zh: 雋不疑字曼倩，勃海人也。

translations.literal: Jun Buyi, courtesy name Manqian, was a man of Bohai.
translations.idiomatic: Jun Buyi, courtesy name Manqian, came from Bohai commandery.

sentences.zh: 治《春秋》，為郡文學，進退必以禮，名聞州郡。

translations.literal: He studied the Spring and Autumn Annals, became the commandery erudite; advancing and withdrawing he always used ritual; his name was heard in the province and commanderies.
translations.idiomatic: He mastered the Spring and Autumn Annals, served as his commandery's erudite, and comported himself with such punctilious courtesy that his reputation spread through the province.

sentences.zh: 武帝末，郡國盜賊群起，暴勝之為直指使者，衣繡衣，持斧，逐捕盜賊，督課郡國，東至海，以軍興誅不從命者，威振州郡。

translations.literal: Near the end of Emperor Wu, bandits rose in crowds in the commanderies and states; Bao Shengzhi became commissioner with direct mandate, wore embroidered robes, held an axe, pursued and captured bandits, supervised and assessed commanderies and states, east to the sea; by military exigency he executed those who did not obey orders; his awe shook province and commanderies.
translations.idiomatic: Late in Emperor Wu's reign bandits sprang up everywhere; Bao Shengzhi was named commissioner with plenipotentiary powers, robed in silk and carrying the imperial axe, hunted down outlaws from the sea westward, enforced the law with military rigor, and cowed every circuit he touched.

sentences.zh: 勝之素聞不疑賢，至勃海，遣吏請與相見。

translations.literal: Shengzhi had long heard Buyi was worthy; when he reached Bohai he sent an official to request a meeting.
translations.idiomatic: Shengzhi had long heard of Buyi's worth; on reaching Bohai he sent an officer to ask for an interview.

sentences.zh: 不疑冠進賢冠，帶櫑具劍，佩環玦，褒衣博帶，盛服至門上謁。

translations.literal: Buyi capped himself with the cap for advancing the worthy, girded the stacked-rack sword, wore ring and jade pendant, loose robe and broad sash, in full dress arrived at the gate and submitted his visiting card.
translations.idiomatic: Buyi appeared in the cap of the worthy candidate, the long sword at his belt, jade ring and pendant, wide sleeves and flowing sash, and presented himself at the gate in full court dress.

sentences.zh: 門下欲使解劍，不疑曰：「劍者，君子武備，所以衛身，不可解。

translations.literal: The gate staff wished to make him ungird his sword; Buyi said: "The sword is the gentleman's martial equipment, whereby to guard the person; it cannot be ungirded.
translations.idiomatic: The porters asked him to leave his sword outside. Buyi replied, "The sword is a gentleman's arms; it guards his person and must stay at his side.

sentences.zh: 請退。」

translations.literal: I request to withdraw."
translations.idiomatic: Then I shall withdraw."

sentences.zh: 吏白勝之。

translations.literal: The official reported to Shengzhi.
translations.idiomatic: The officer reported this to Shengzhi.

sentences.zh: 勝之開閣延請，望見不疑容貌尊嚴，衣冠甚偉，勝之□履起迎。

translations.literal: Shengzhi opened the pavilion gate and extended invitation; gazing he saw Buyi's countenance solemn and dignified, cap and robes very grand; Shengzhi (text damaged) slippers and rose to welcome.
translations.idiomatic: Shengzhi threw open the door to receive him; one look at Buyi's stern bearing and magnificent dress sent him scrambling from his seat—one character missing in the text—to greet him.

sentences.zh: 登堂坐定，不疑據地曰：「竊伏海瀕，聞暴公子威名舊矣，今乃承顏接辭。

translations.literal: They ascended the hall and sat settled; Buyi knelt on the ground and said: "I have humbly lain by the sea's edge; I have heard Lord Bao's awesome name long; now I receive face and connect words.
translations.idiomatic: When they were seated in the hall, Buyi knelt and said, "I have lived obscurely by the coast and long admired your lordship's name; today I am honored to look upon you and speak with you.

sentences.zh: 凡為吏，太剛則折，太柔則廢，威行施之以恩，然後樹功揚名，永終天祿。」

translations.literal: In general as an official, too rigid then snaps, too soft then is useless; awe in conduct apply it with kindness; then afterward establish merit and raise name, forever finish heaven's stipend."
translations.idiomatic: For any magistrate, too much rigidity snaps him, too much softness undoes him; temper stern rule with kindness, and only then can he win lasting merit, a good name, and the blessings of office."

sentences.zh: 勝之知不疑非庸人，敬納其戒，深接以禮意，問當世所施行。

translations.literal: Shengzhi knew Buyi was no ordinary man; respectfully he accepted his warning, deeply received him with ritual intent, asked what was practiced in the present age.
translations.idiomatic: Shengzhi saw he was no common man, took his counsel to heart, treated him with full courtesy, and asked his views on current policy.

sentences.zh: 門下諸從事皆州郡選吏，側聽不疑，莫不驚駭。

translations.literal: The various attendants under the gate were all province-and-commandery-selected clerks; listening sideways to Buyi, none were not startled and awestruck.
translations.idiomatic: The staff—all picked men from the provinces—listened in stunned silence.

sentences.zh: 至昏夜，罷去。

translations.literal: Until dusk and night, they stopped and departed.
translations.idiomatic: They talked on until nightfall before breaking up.

sentences.zh: 勝之遂表薦不疑，征詣公車，拜為青州刺史。

translations.literal: Shengzhi then memorialized recommending Buyi; he was summoned to the public carriage gate and appointed Governor of Qingzhou.
translations.idiomatic: Shengzhi memorialized his name; the court summoned him through the public carriage office and named him Governor of Qingzhou.

sentences.zh: 始元五年，有一男子乘黃犢車，建黃旐，衣黃襜褕，著黃冒，詣北闕，自謂衛太子。

translations.literal: In the fifth year of Shiyuan, there was a man who rode a yellow calf cart, raised a yellow banner, wore a yellow short coat, put on a yellow cap, went to the northern gate tower, calling himself the Heir Apparent of Wei.
translations.idiomatic: In Shiyuan 5 a man arrived at the north gate in a yellow cart under a yellow banner, dressed in yellow from cap to coat, and announced that he was the crown prince of Wei.

sentences.zh: 公車以聞，詔使公卿、將軍、中二千石雜識視。

translations.literal: The public carriage office reported it; an edict ordered excellencies, generals, and full-two-thousand-shi officials jointly to identify and inspect.
translations.idiomatic: The public carriage office reported the matter, and the emperor ordered high ministers, generals, and officials at the two-thousand-shi rank to go and identify him.

sentences.zh: 長安中吏民聚觀者數萬人。

translations.literal: In Chang'an officials and commoners who gathered to watch were several tens of thousands.
translations.idiomatic: Tens of thousands of officials and commoners mobbed the streets of Chang'an to watch.

sentences.zh: 右將軍勒兵闕下，以備非常。

translations.literal: The General of the Right marshaled troops below the gate tower, to guard against the extraordinary.
translations.idiomatic: The General of the Right drew up troops beneath the gate in case of trouble.

sentences.zh: 丞相、御史、中二千石至者並莫敢發言。

translations.literal: The chancellor, imperial counselor, and those full-two-thousand-shi who arrived together none dared speak.
translations.idiomatic: The chancellor, the imperial counselor, and every two-thousand-shi official on the scene kept silent.

sentences.zh: 京兆尹不疑後到，叱從吏收縛。

translations.literal: Governor of the Capital Buyi arrived afterward; he shouted to following clerks to seize and bind.
translations.idiomatic: Jun Buyi, Governor of the Capital, arrived last and ordered his men to arrest the man at once.

sentences.zh: 或曰：「是非未可知，且安之。」

translations.literal: Someone said: "Right and wrong are not yet knowable; for the moment settle him."
translations.idiomatic: Someone urged him to wait: "We cannot yet be sure—go slowly."

sentences.zh: 不疑曰：「諸君何患於衛太子！

translations.literal: Buyi said: "Why do you gentlemen worry about the Heir Apparent of Wei!
translations.idiomatic: Buyi replied, "Why should you fear the crown prince of Wei?

sentences.zh: 昔蒯聵違命出奔，輒距而不納，《春秋》是之。

translations.literal: Formerly Kuai Kui disobeyed orders and fled out; Zhe kept him off and would not admit him; the Spring and Autumn Annals approved this.
translations.idiomatic: When Prince Kuai Kui fled Wei, his son refused him entry, and the Spring and Autumn praises that refusal.

sentences.zh: 衛太子得罪先帝，亡不即死，今來自詣，此罪人也。」

translations.literal: The Heir Apparent of Wei offended the former emperor, fled and did not immediately die; now he has come of himself to present himself—this is a criminal."
translations.idiomatic: The crown prince of Wei wronged the late emperor and ran away instead of dying for it; whoever walks in now is a criminal impostor."

sentences.zh: 遂送詔獄。

translations.literal: Thereupon he was sent to the edict prison.
translations.idiomatic: He was marched off to the imperial prison.

sentences.zh: 天子與大將軍霍光聞而嘉之，曰：「公卿大臣當用經術明於大誼。」

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven and Grand General Huo Guang heard and praised him, saying: "Excellencies and great ministers ought to use classical learning to clarify the great principle."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor and Grand General Huo Guang praised him, declaring that ministers should settle great questions with the classics.

sentences.zh: 由是名聲重於朝廷，在位者皆自以不及也。

translations.literal: From this his name and sound were weighty at court; those in place all thought themselves not equal.
translations.idiomatic: His name rang through the court, and every man in office felt himself outmatched.

sentences.zh: 大將軍光欲以女妻之，不疑固辭，不肯當。

translations.literal: Grand General Guang wished to give him his daughter as wife; Buyi firmly declined, would not accept.
translations.idiomatic: Huo Guang offered him a daughter in marriage, but Buyi steadfastly refused.

sentences.zh: 久之，以病免，終於家。

translations.literal: After a long time, by illness he was dismissed, ended at home.
translations.idiomatic: In time he resigned on grounds of illness and died at home.

sentences.zh: 京師紀之。

translations.literal: The capital recorded it.
translations.idiomatic: The capital long remembered the deed.

sentences.zh: 後趙廣漢為京兆尹，言：「我禁奸止邪，行於吏民，至於朝廷事，不及不疑遠甚。」

translations.literal: Later Zhao Guanghan was Governor of the Capital; he said: "I forbid wickedness and stop perversity, practice it on clerks and commoners; as to court affairs, I do not reach Buyi by far."
translations.idiomatic: Later Zhao Guanghan, as Governor of the Capital, said, "I can curb crime among officials and commoners, but beside Buyi I am nothing when it comes to affairs of state."

sentences.zh: 廷尉驗治何人，竟得奸詐。

translations.literal: The commandant of justice examined and prosecuted what man; in the end obtained deceit and fraud.
translations.idiomatic: The commandant of justice investigated and exposed the imposture.

sentences.zh: 本夏陽人，姓成名方遂，居湖，以卜筮為事。

translations.literal: Originally a man of Xiayang, surname Cheng named Fangsu, dwelt at Hu, by divination made his occupation.
translations.idiomatic: He was a native of Xiayang named Cheng Fangsu, who lived at Hu and earned his living as a diviner.

sentences.zh: 有故太子舍人嘗從方遂卜，謂曰：「子狀貌甚似衛太子。」

translations.literal: There was a former heir-apparent household gentleman who once followed Fangsu for divination, said to him: "Your form and appearance very resemble the Heir Apparent of Wei."
translations.idiomatic: A former gentleman-attendant of the heir apparent had once consulted him and remarked, "You look remarkably like the crown prince of Wei."

sentences.zh: 方遂心利其言，幾得以富貴，即詐自稱詣闕，廷尉逮召鄉里知識者張宗祿等，方遂坐誣罔不道，要斬東市。

translations.literal: Fangsu in his heart profited by these words, almost was able thereby to obtain wealth and honor; immediately falsely called himself and went to the gate tower; the commandant of justice arrested and summoned Zhang Zonglu and others who knew him in village and neighborhood; Fangsu sat for deceitful falsehood lacking the Way, was cut in two at the waist at the eastern market.
translations.idiomatic: Fangsu seized on the idea, hoping for rank and riches, and presented himself at the palace gates under a false name. The commandant of justice rounded up witnesses such as Zhang Zonglu; Fangsu was convicted of outrageous imposture and beheaded in the eastern market.

sentences.zh: 一雲姓張名延年。

translations.literal: One version says surname Zhang named Yannian.
translations.idiomatic: Another tradition gives his name as Zhang Yannian.

sentences.zh: 疏廣字仲翁，東海蘭陵人也。

translations.literal: Shu Guang, courtesy name Zhongweng, was a man of Lanling in Donghai.
translations.idiomatic: Shu Guang, courtesy name Zhongweng, came from Lanling in Donghai commandery.

sentences.zh: 少好學，明《春秋》，家居教授，學者自遠方至。

translations.literal: In youth he loved learning, understood the Spring and Autumn Annals; at home he dwelt teaching, scholars from distant places came.
translations.idiomatic: He loved learning from boyhood, mastered the Spring and Autumn Annals, and taught at home until students came from afar.

sentences.zh: 徵為博士、太中大夫。

translations.literal: He was summoned as erudite and Grand Counselor of the Palace.
translations.idiomatic: The court summoned him as an erudite and then as Grand Counselor of the Palace.

sentences.zh: 地節三年，立皇太子，選丙吉為太傅，廣為少傅，數月，吉遷御史大夫，廣徙為太傅。

translations.literal: In the third year of Dijie, the imperial heir was established; Bing Ji was selected as grand tutor, Guang as junior tutor; after several months Ji was promoted commandant of the imperial counselors, Guang was transferred to be grand tutor.
translations.idiomatic: In Dijie 3, when the heir apparent was installed, Bing Ji became grand tutor and Shu Guang junior tutor; a few months later Ji rose to commandant of the imperial counselors and Guang succeeded him as grand tutor.

sentences.zh: 廣兄子受字公子，亦以賢良舉為太子家令。

translations.literal: Guang's elder brother's son Shou, courtesy name Gongzi, also by worthy and good was recommended as heir-apparent household superintendent.
translations.idiomatic: His nephew Shu Shou, courtesy name Gongzi, was likewise recommended on the worthy-and-good list and made superintendent of the heir's household.

sentences.zh: 受好禮恭謹，敏而有辭。

translations.literal: Shou loved ritual, was respectful and careful, keen and had words.
translations.idiomatic: Shu Shou loved ritual, was deferential and careful, quick-witted and eloquent.

sentences.zh: 宣帝幸太子宮，受迎謁應對，及置酒宴，奉觴上壽，辭禮閑雅，上甚歡說。

translations.literal: Emperor Xuan visited the heir's palace; Shou greeted, visited, answered; when they set wine and feast, he raised the cup and offered longevity; his phrasing and ritual were leisurely and elegant; the sovereign was very pleased.
translations.idiomatic: When Emperor Xuan visited the heir's palace, Shou received him with flawless courtesy; at the banquet he raised the toast with such grace that the emperor was delighted.

sentences.zh: 頃之，拜受為少傅。

translations.literal: Before long, Shou was appointed junior tutor.
translations.idiomatic: Soon afterward he was named junior tutor.

sentences.zh: 太子外祖父特進平恩侯許伯以為太子少，白使其弟中郎將舜監護太子家。

translations.literal: The heir's maternal grandfather, specially advanced Marquis of Ping'en Xu Bo, thought the heir young, reported and made his younger brother General of the Household Shun supervise and guard the heir's household.
translations.idiomatic: The heir's maternal grandfather, Marquis of Ping'en Xu Bo, thought the boy needed more minders and asked that his brother, General of the Household Xu Shun, oversee the heir's establishment.

sentences.zh: 上以問廣，廣對曰：「太子國儲副君，師友必於天下英俊，不宜獨親外家許氏。

translations.literal: The sovereign thereon asked Guang; Guang replied: "The heir is the state's stored successor-lord; teachers and friends must be from the realm's outstanding talents; it is not fitting to be intimate only with the maternal kin Xu clan.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor consulted Shu Guang, who answered, "The heir is the nation's reserve; his teachers and companions should be the finest men in the empire, not drawn chiefly from the Xu in-laws.

sentences.zh: 且太子自有太傅、少傅。

translations.literal: Moreover the heir already has grand tutor and junior tutor.
translations.idiomatic: Besides, he already has a grand tutor and a junior tutor.

sentences.zh: 官屬已備，今復使舜護太子家，視陋，非所以廣太子德於天下也。」

translations.literal: Official subordinates are already complete; now again to make Shun guard the heir's household shows meanness; it is not the means whereby to broaden the heir's virtue in the realm."
translations.idiomatic: His staff is complete; to add Xu Shun as overseer looks petty and does nothing to spread the heir's virtue through the realm."

sentences.zh: 上善其言，以語丞相魏相，相免冠謝曰：「此非臣等所能及。」

translations.literal: The sovereign approved his words, told Chancellor Wei Xiang; Xiang doffed cap and apologized saying: "This is not what your servants could reach."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor approved and repeated the remark to Chancellor Wei Xiang, who removed his cap and said, "We could never have risen to that."

sentences.zh: 廣由是見器重，數受賞賜。

translations.literal: Guang from this was seen as weighty and trusted, repeatedly received rewards and gifts.
translations.idiomatic: From then on Guang stood high in favor and often received imperial gifts.

sentences.zh: 太子每朝，因進見，太傅在前，少傅在後。

translations.literal: Whenever the heir attended court, thereby advancing to audience, grand tutor in front, junior tutor behind.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever the heir attended court, the grand tutor walked before him and the junior tutor behind.

sentences.zh: 父子並為師傅，朝廷以為榮。

translations.literal: Uncle and nephew together were tutors; the court thought it glorious.
translations.idiomatic: Uncle and nephew serving together as the heir's tutors was counted an honor throughout the court.

sentences.zh: 在位五歲，皇太子年十二，通《論語》、《孝經》。

translations.literal: In place five years, the imperial heir was twelve years old, comprehended the Analects and Classic of Filial Piety.
translations.idiomatic: After five years in office the heir was twelve and had mastered the Analects and the Classic of Filial Piety.

sentences.zh: 廣謂受曰：「吾聞『知足不辱，知止不殆』，『功遂身退，天之道』也。

translations.literal: Guang said to Shou: "I have heard 'knowing sufficiency is not disgraced, knowing stopping is not endangered,' 'merit accomplished, body withdrawing—that is heaven's way.'
translations.idiomatic: Guang said to Shou, "I have read that contentment saves a man from shame, that knowing when to stop keeps him from harm, and that to retire once the deed is done is heaven's own way."

sentences.zh: 今仕官至二千石，宦成名立，如此不去，懼有後悔，豈如父子相隨出關，歸老故鄉，以壽命終，不亦善乎？」

translations.literal: Now official rank has reached two thousand shi, office accomplished name established; if thus we do not depart, I fear there will be later regret—how compare to uncle and nephew following each other out the passes, returning old to native village, ending by lifespan—is it not also good?"
translations.idiomatic: We stand at the two-thousand-shi rank with name and office secure; if we stay on we may live to regret it. Better that uncle and nephew leave the capital together, go home to grow old, and die in our own beds—would that not be the better course?"

sentences.zh: 受叩頭曰：「從大人議。」

translations.literal: Shou knocked head saying: "I follow the great man's deliberation."
translations.idiomatic: Shou kowtowed and said, "I obey your counsel, uncle."

sentences.zh: 即日父子俱移病。

translations.literal: That day uncle and nephew together feigned illness.
translations.idiomatic: That same day both men reported themselves ill.

sentences.zh: 滿三月賜告，廣遂稱篤，上疏乞骸骨。

translations.literal: Full three months granted leave; Guang then claimed grave illness, submitted memorial begging bones.
translations.idiomatic: After three months' sick leave Guang declared his illness grave and memorialized for permission to retire.

sentences.zh: 上以其年篤老，皆許之，加賜黃金二十斤，皇太子贈以五十斤。

translations.literal: The sovereign because their years were grave and old, all permitted it, additionally bestowed twenty jin of gold; the imperial heir gifted fifty jin.
translations.idiomatic: Considering their age, the emperor granted the request, added twenty jin of gold from his own purse, and the heir sent fifty more.

sentences.zh: 公卿大夫故人邑子設祖道，供張東都門外，送者車數百兩，辭決而去。

translations.literal: Excellencies, grandees, old friends, and townsmen set the road-offering feast, spread provisions outside the eastern capital gate; those who sent off were several hundred carriages; they took leave and parted and departed.
translations.idiomatic: High officials, old friends, and fellow townsmen staged a farewell banquet outside the eastern gate; hundreds of carts lined the road as the two men took their leave and set out.

sentences.zh: 及道路觀者皆曰：「賢哉二大夫！」

translations.literal: And those who watched on the road all said: "How worthy the two grandees!"
translations.idiomatic: Onlookers along the way cried, "What worthy men these two grandees are!"

sentences.zh: 或歎息為之下泣。

translations.literal: Some sighed and for them shed tears below.
translations.idiomatic: Some sighed and wept for them.

sentences.zh: 廣既歸鄉里，日令家共具設酒食，請族人故舊賓客，與相娛樂。

translations.literal: Guang having returned to village and neighborhood, daily ordered the household together to prepare setting wine and food, invited clansmen, old associates, and guests, with them mutually enjoyed and delighted.
translations.idiomatic: Back in his home district Guang had his household lay a feast every day for kinsmen, old friends, and guests, and joined them in good cheer.

sentences.zh: 數問其家金余尚有幾所，趣賣以共具。

translations.literal: Repeatedly asked his household how much gold remainder still had several places, urged selling to supply the preparations.
translations.idiomatic: He often asked how much gold was left and urged his people to sell some whenever supplies ran low.

sentences.zh: 居歲餘，廣子孫竊謂其昆弟老人廣所愛信者曰：「子孫幾及君時頗立產業基址，今日飲食，費且盡。

translations.literal: Dwelling more than a year, Guang's sons and grandsons secretly spoke to their elder brothers and old men whom Guang loved and trusted, saying: "Sons and grandsons almost reached your time and somewhat established property foundations; now daily drink and food, expense will soon be exhausted.
translations.idiomatic: After a year his descendants quietly said to the old kinsmen Guang trusted, "We hoped to build up land and houses while you still lived, but these feasts are eating the fortune away.

sentences.zh: 宜從丈人所，勸說君買田宅。」

translations.literal: It is fitting to follow the elder's place, urge and persuade the lord to buy fields and houses."
translations.idiomatic: Ask our elders to urge him to buy land and a house."

sentences.zh: 老人即以閒暇時為廣言此計，廣曰：「吾凱老悖不念子孫哉？

translations.literal: The old men then in idle leisure time for Guang spoke this plan; Guang said: "Am I (character 凱, possibly scribal error for 豈 'how could') old and perverse, not thinking of sons and grandsons?
translations.idiomatic: When the elders relayed the plea, Guang said, "Do you take me for a senile fool who no longer cares for his descendants?"

sentences.zh: 顧自有舊田廬，令子孫勤力其中，足以共衣食，與凡人齊。

translations.literal: Only consider we already have old fields and huts; order sons and grandsons to labor diligently therein, enough to supply clothes and food, equal with ordinary men.
translations.idiomatic: They already have our old fields and cottages; if they work them hard they can dress and eat like any other family.

sentences.zh: 今復增益之以為贏余，但教子孫怠惰耳。

translations.literal: Now again to increase and add to them to make surplus, only teaches sons and grandsons laziness.
translations.idiomatic: To pile up more for them now would only teach them idleness.

sentences.zh: 賢而多財，則捐其志；

translations.literal: If worthy and much wealth, then casts away their resolve;
translations.idiomatic: Talent plus a fat purse erodes ambition;

sentences.zh: 愚而多財，則益其過。

translations.literal: if foolish and much wealth, then increases their faults.
translations.idiomatic: foolishness plus wealth only magnifies fault.

sentences.zh: 且夫富者，眾人之怨也；

translations.literal: Moreover as for the rich, they are the multitude's resentment;
translations.idiomatic: Besides, riches draw the world's resentment;

sentences.zh: 吾既亡以教化子孫，不欲益其過而生怨。

translations.literal: I already lack means to teach and transform sons and grandsons; I do not wish to increase their faults and engender resentment.
translations.idiomatic: I have no new wisdom to pass on; I will not swell their faults and make enemies for them.

sentences.zh: 又此金者，聖主所以惠養老臣也，故樂與鄉黨宗族共饗其賜，以盡吾餘日，不亦可乎！」

translations.literal: Moreover this gold is what the sage ruler used to favor and nurture old ministers; therefore I am pleased with village, party, and clan jointly to feast on this gift, thereby to exhaust my remaining days—is it not also acceptable!"
translations.idiomatic: This gold is the emperor's gift to an old servant; I mean to share it with neighbors and kin and toast away my last years—what could be better?"

sentences.zh: 於是族人說服。

translations.literal: Thereupon the clansmen were pleased and submitted.
translations.idiomatic: The clan accepted his reasoning.

sentences.zh: 皆以壽終。

translations.literal: All by longevity ended.
translations.idiomatic: Both men lived out their full span.

sentences.zh: 于定國

translations.literal: Yu Dingguo.
translations.idiomatic: Yu Dingguo

sentences.zh: 于定國字曼倩，東海郯人也。

translations.literal: Yu Dingguo, courtesy name Manqian, was a man of Tan in Donghai.
translations.idiomatic: Yu Dingguo, courtesy name Manqian, came from Tan in Donghai commandery.

sentences.zh: 其父於公為縣獄吏、郡決曹，決獄平，羅文法者於公所決皆不恨。

translations.literal: His father Lord Yu was county prison clerk and commandery decision-court clerk; he decided prison cases fairly; those who fell under the written law in Lord Yu's decisions all did not resent.
translations.idiomatic: His father, known as Lord Yu, was a county prison clerk and a commandery sentencing officer; his verdicts were so fair that even those he condemned under the statutes felt no bitterness.

sentences.zh: 郡中為之生立祠，號曰于公祠。

translations.literal: In the commandery for him while living they erected a shrine, called it Lord Yu's shrine.
translations.idiomatic: The commandery built him a shrine while he still lived—the Shrine of Lord Yu.

sentences.zh: 東海有孝婦，少寡，亡子，養姑甚謹，姑欲嫁之，終不肯。

translations.literal: In Donghai there was a filial daughter-in-law; young she was widowed, lost a son, nourished her mother-in-law very carefully; the mother-in-law wished to marry her off; she in the end would not consent.
translations.idiomatic: Donghai had a famously dutiful daughter-in-law: widowed young and childless, she cared for her mother-in-law with devotion; when the old woman tried to marry her off, she refused.

sentences.zh: 姑謂鄰人曰：「孝婦事我勤苦，哀其亡子守寡。

translations.literal: The mother-in-law said to neighbors: "The filial daughter-in-law serves me diligent and bitter; I pity her losing a son and guarding widowhood.
translations.idiomatic: The mother-in-law told a neighbor, "She slaves for me out of pity—she lost her child and stays a widow for my sake.

sentences.zh: 我老，久累丁壯，奈何？」

translations.literal: I am old, long have burdened a stalwart adult—what to do?"
translations.idiomatic: I am old and have tied down a young woman—what am I to do?"

sentences.zh: 其後姑自經死，姑女告吏：「婦殺我母」。

translations.literal: Afterward the mother-in-law hanged herself; the mother-in-law's daughter reported to clerks: "The daughter-in-law killed my mother."
translations.idiomatic: Later the mother-in-law hanged herself, and the old woman's daughter told the magistrate, "The daughter-in-law murdered my mother."

sentences.zh: 吏捕孝婦，孝婦辭不殺姑。

translations.literal: The clerks arrested the filial daughter-in-law; the filial daughter-in-law pleaded she did not kill the mother-in-law.
translations.idiomatic: Officers seized the daughter-in-law, who swore she had not harmed her mother-in-law.

sentences.zh: 吏驗治，孝婦自誣服。

translations.literal: The clerks examined and prosecuted; the filial daughter-in-law herself falsely confessed submission.
translations.idiomatic: Under interrogation she broke and confessed to a crime she had not committed.

sentences.zh: 具獄上府，于公以為此婦養姑十餘年，以孝聞，必不殺也。

translations.literal: The complete case was sent up to the headquarters; Lord Yu thought this woman had nourished her mother-in-law more than ten years, was heard for filiality, surely did not kill.
translations.idiomatic: When the file reached headquarters, Lord Yu argued that a woman famed for more than ten years of care could not have killed her.

sentences.zh: 太守不聽，于公爭之，弗能得，乃抱其具獄，哭於府上，因辭疾去。

translations.literal: The grand administrator would not listen; Lord Yu disputed it, could not obtain his point, then embraced the complete case dossier, wept at the headquarters gate, thereupon declined illness and departed.
translations.idiomatic: The grand administrator would not hear him; Lord Yu wept on the steps with the dossier in his arms, then resigned on grounds of illness.

sentences.zh: 太守竟論殺孝婦。

translations.literal: The grand administrator in the end judged and killed the filial daughter-in-law.
translations.idiomatic: In the end the grand administrator had the innocent woman executed.

sentences.zh: 郡中枯旱三年。

translations.literal: In the commandery drought parched three years.
translations.idiomatic: For three years the commandery withered in drought.

sentences.zh: 後太守至，卜筮其故，於公曰：「孝婦不當死，前太守強斷之，咎黨在是乎？」

translations.literal: Later a grand administrator arrived, divined the cause; Lord Yu said: "The filial daughter-in-law ought not to have died; the former grand administrator forcibly judged her; is the fault's party here?"
translations.idiomatic: A new grand administrator cast divinations to learn the cause. Lord Yu said, "That woman should never have died; the last magistrate forced a verdict—perhaps the blame lies there?"

sentences.zh: 於是太守殺牛自祭孝婦塚，因表其墓，天立大雨，歲孰。

translations.literal: Thereupon the grand administrator killed an ox and himself sacrificed at the filial daughter-in-law's tomb, thereby marked her grave; heaven immediately great rain; the year ripened.
translations.idiomatic: The magistrate sacrificed an ox at her grave and had the tomb marked; rain fell at once and the harvest ripened.

sentences.zh: 郡中以此大敬重于公。

translations.literal: In the commandery because of this greatly revered Lord Yu.
translations.idiomatic: From that day the commandery revered Lord Yu as a prophet of justice.

sentences.zh: 定國乃迎師學《春秋》，身執經，北面備弟子禮。

translations.literal: Dingguo then welcomed a teacher to study the Spring and Autumn Annals; personally he held the classics, faced north and prepared disciple ritual.
translations.idiomatic: Dingguo took a tutor in the Spring and Autumn Annals, clasped the text in his own hands, and sat as a pupil facing north.

sentences.zh: 為人廉恭，尤重經術士，雖卑賤徒步往過，定國皆與鈞禮，恩敬甚備，學士咸稱焉。

translations.literal: As a person he was incorrupt and respectful, especially valued classicist scholars; though lowly and vile, on foot going to visit, Dingguo all gave them equal ritual, kindness and respect very complete; scholars all praised him.
translations.idiomatic: He was modest and scrupulous and honored classicists; even a poor scholar who walked to his door received the same courtesy as a noble guest, and men of learning sang his praises.

sentences.zh: 其決疑平法，務在哀鰥寡，罪疑從輕。

translations.literal: In his deciding doubt and leveling law, he strove to pity widowers and widows; when guilt was doubtful, follow the light.
translations.idiomatic: In doubtful cases he leaned toward mercy for the widow and the orphan and always chose the lighter penalty when the facts were unclear.

sentences.zh: 加審慎之心。

translations.literal: Added careful and cautious mind.
translations.idiomatic: He added a painstaking care of his own.

sentences.zh: 朝廷稱之曰：「張釋之為廷尉，天下無冤民；

translations.literal: The court praised him saying: "When Zhang Shizhi was commandant of justice, the realm had no wronged commoners;
translations.idiomatic: The court said of him, "When Zhang Shizhi held the seal of the commandant of justice, no innocent man languished in prison;

sentences.zh: 於定國為廷尉，民自以不冤。」

translations.literal: Yu Dingguo became commandant of justice, the people themselves thought they were not wronged."
translations.idiomatic: under Yu Dingguo as commandant of justice the common folk still believed themselves free of injustice."

sentences.zh: 定國食酒至數石不亂，冬月治請讞，飲酒益精明。

translations.literal: Dingguo consumed wine to several stone without disorder; in winter months when handling submitted appeals for review, drinking wine he became still more keen and clear.
translations.idiomatic: Dingguo could drink several stone of wine without losing his wits; in the deep of winter, when he reviewed capital appeals, a cup seemed only to sharpen his judgment.

sentences.zh: 為廷尉十八歲，遷御史大夫。

translations.literal: As commandant of justice eighteen years, he was promoted commandant of the imperial counselors.
translations.idiomatic: After eighteen years as commandant of justice he rose to commandant of the imperial counselors.

sentences.zh: 子永嗣。

translations.literal: His son Yong succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: His son Yu Yong inherited his title.

sentences.zh: 少時，耆酒多過失，年且三十，乃折節修行，以父任為侍中中郎將、長水校尉。

translations.literal: In youth he loved wine and had many faults; when his years approached thirty, then he broke his bent and cultivated conduct; by father's privilege he became palace attendant, General of the Household, and Colonel of the Changshui Archers.
translations.idiomatic: In youth he drank hard and often stumbled; near thirty he mended his ways, and through his father's rank became a palace attendant, General of the Household, and Colonel of the Changshui Archers.

sentences.zh: 定國死，居喪如禮，孝行聞。

translations.literal: When Dingguo died, he dwelt mourning according to ritual; filial conduct was heard.
translations.idiomatic: When Dingguo died, he observed the full mourning rites and his filial piety became known.

sentences.zh: 由是以列侯為散騎、光祿勳，至御史大夫。

translations.literal: From this as full marquis he became attendant at leisure and superintendent of the imperial household, reaching commandant of the imperial counselors.
translations.idiomatic: He rose from full marquis to attendant at leisure and superintendent of the imperial household, then to commandant of the imperial counselors.

sentences.zh: 尚館陶公主施。

translations.literal: He married Princess Guantao Shi.
translations.idiomatic: He was given in marriage to Princess Guantao, whose personal name was Shi.

sentences.zh: 施者，宣帝長女，成帝姑也，賢有行，永以選尚焉。

translations.literal: Shi was Emperor Xuan's eldest daughter, Emperor Cheng's aunt; worthy, had conduct; Yong by selection married her.
translations.idiomatic: Shi was Emperor Xuan's eldest daughter and Emperor Cheng's aunt, a woman of virtue and good repute; Yong was chosen as her consort.

sentences.zh: 上方欲相之，會永薨。

translations.literal: The sovereign was about to make him chancellor when it happened Yong died.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor was on the point of making him chancellor when Yu Yong died.

sentences.zh: 子恬嗣。

translations.literal: His son Tian succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: His son Yu Tian inherited the line.

sentences.zh: 恬不肖，薄於行。

translations.literal: Tian was not like his forebears, thin in conduct.
translations.idiomatic: Yu Tian proved unworthy and mean in his conduct.

sentences.zh: 始，定國父于公，其閭門壞，父老方共治之。

translations.literal: At the beginning, Dingguo's father Lord Yu, their lane gate was ruined; the village elders were jointly repairing it.
translations.idiomatic: Long before, when Dingguo's father Lord Yu was alive, the gate of their lane fell into ruin and the elders of the village were repairing it together.

sentences.zh: 于公謂曰：「少高大閭門，令容駟馬高蓋車。

translations.literal: Lord Yu said to them: "Slightly raise high the lane gate, make it admit a four-horse high-canopy carriage.
translations.idiomatic: Lord Yu told them, "Raise the gate high enough for a four-horse carriage with a tall canopy to pass through.

sentences.zh: 我治獄多陰德，未嘗有所冤，子孫必有興者。」

translations.literal: In my judging cases I have much hidden virtue, never have had wrongful judgments; sons and grandsons surely will have one who rises."
translations.idiomatic: I have tried cases with a mind to hidden merit and have never knowingly wronged a soul; my descendants will flourish for it."

sentences.zh: 至定國為丞相，永為御史大夫，封侯傳世雲。

translations.literal: Down to Dingguo becoming chancellor, Yong becoming commandant of the imperial counselors, enfeoffed marquis passing generations—it is said.
translations.idiomatic: In the end Dingguo became chancellor and Yong commandant of the imperial counselors, both enfeoffed as marquises whose lines endured for generations.

sentences.zh: 薛廣德

translations.literal: Xue Guangde.
translations.idiomatic: Xue Guangde

sentences.zh: 薛廣德字長卿，沛郡相人也。

translations.literal: Xue Guangde, courtesy name Changqing, was a man of Xiang in Pei commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Xue Guangde, courtesy name Changqing, came from Xiang in Pei commandery.

sentences.zh: 以《魯詩》教授楚國，龔勝、捨師事焉。

translations.literal: By the Lu version of the Classic of Songs he instructed the state of Chu; Gong Sheng and She took him as teacher to serve.
translations.idiomatic: He taught the Lu recension of the Classic of Songs in the state of Chu; Gong Sheng and Gong She became his disciples.

sentences.zh: 蕭望之為御史大夫，除廣德為屬，數與論議，器之，薦廣德經行宜充本朝。

translations.literal: When Xiao Wangzhi was commandant of the imperial counselors, he appointed Guangde as subordinate, repeatedly debated with him, valued him, recommended Guangde's classical learning and conduct as fitting to fill this court.
translations.idiomatic: When Xiao Wangzhi was commandant of the imperial counselors he took Guangde onto his staff, debated policy with him often, admired him, and recommended his learning and character as fit for high office at court.

sentences.zh: 為博士，論石渠，遷諫大夫，代貢禹為長信少府、御史大夫。

translations.literal: He became erudite, debated at Stone Canal, was promoted remonstrance grandee, replaced Gong Yu as superintendent of the Changle Palace and commandant of the imperial counselors.
translations.idiomatic: He served as an erudite, took part in the Stone Canal conference, became remonstrance grandee, then succeeded Gong Yu as superintendent of the Changle Palace and as commandant of the imperial counselors.

sentences.zh: 廣德為人溫雅有醞藉。

translations.literal: Guangde as a person was mild and elegant, had depth and reserve.
translations.idiomatic: Guangde was gentle, refined, and self-contained.

sentences.zh: 及為三公，直言諫爭。

translations.literal: When he became one of the three dukes, he spoke straight remonstrance and disputed.
translations.idiomatic: Once he sat among the three dukes he spoke bluntly in remonstrance.

sentences.zh: 始拜旬日間，上幸甘泉，郊泰時畤，禮畢，因留射獵。

translations.literal: Within ten days after first appointment, the sovereign visited Sweet Springs, suburban-sacrificed at the Taiyi and Tai altars; when ritual was finished, thereby stayed to shoot and hunt.
translations.idiomatic: Within days of his appointment the emperor went to Sweet Springs to sacrifice at the Taiyi and Tai shrines and then lingered to hunt.

sentences.zh: 廣德上書曰：「竊見關東困極，人民流離。

translations.literal: Guangde submitted memorial saying: "I humbly see east of the passes exhausted to the limit, the people scattered and drifting.
translations.idiomatic: Guangde memorialized: "East of the passes the people are destitute and driven from their homes.

sentences.zh: 陛下日撞亡秦之鐘，聽鄭、衛之樂，臣誠悼之。

translations.literal: Your majesty daily strikes the bell of perished Qin, listens to Zheng and Wei music—I truly mourn it.
translations.idiomatic: Yet Your Majesty tolls the bell of fallen Qin day after day and fills your ears with the licentious airs of Zheng and Wei—I grieve for it.

sentences.zh: 今士卒暴露，從官勞倦，願隊下亟反官，思與百姓同憂樂，天下幸甚。」

translations.literal: Now soldiers and guards are exposed to the elements, following officials weary; I wish your majesty urgently return to the palace, think to share sorrow and joy with the hundred surnames—great fortune for the realm!"
translations.idiomatic: The troops stand in the open and your attendants are worn out; I beg Your Majesty to return to the palace at once and share the people's weal and woe—that would be the realm's good fortune. The manuscript here confuses two similar characters for the honorific "your majesty."

sentences.zh: 上即日還。

translations.literal: The sovereign that day returned.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor turned back the same day.

sentences.zh: 其秋，上酎祭宗廟，出便門，欲御樓船，廣德當乘輿車，免冠頓首曰：「宜從橋。」

translations.literal: That autumn, the sovereign performed the zhōu sacrifice at the ancestral temple, went out Convenient Gate, wished to mount the tower ship; Guangde blocked the imperial carriage, doffed cap and knocked head saying: "It is fitting to go by the bridge."
translations.idiomatic: That autumn, after the zhōu offering at the ancestral shrines, the emperor left by the Convenient Gate intending to board a tower ship. Guangde planted himself before the chariot, removed his cap, and kowtowed: "You should take the bridge."

sentences.zh: 詔曰：「大夫冠。」

translations.literal: An edict said: "Grandee, cap yourself."
translations.idiomatic: The edict read, "Grandee, put your cap back on."

sentences.zh: 廣德曰：「陛下不聽臣，臣自刎，以血污車輪，陛下不得入廟矣！」

translations.literal: Guangde said: "If your majesty will not heed your servant, your servant will himself cut his throat, with blood defile the carriage wheels—your majesty will not be able to enter the temple!"
translations.idiomatic: Guangde cried, "If you will not hear me I shall cut my throat here and foul your wheels with my blood—you will never enter the temple!"

sentences.zh: 上不說。

translations.literal: The sovereign was not pleased.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor was not pleased.

sentences.zh: 先驅光祿大夫張猛進曰：「臣聞主聖臣直。

translations.literal: The vanguard Imperial Household Grandee Zhang Meng advanced saying: "I have heard the ruler sage, ministers straight.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Meng, Imperial Household Grandee riding in the van, stepped forward: "They say a wise sovereign has blunt ministers.

sentences.zh: 乘船危，就橋安，聖主不乘危。

translations.literal: Riding a ship is perilous; taking the bridge is safe; a sage lord does not ride peril.
translations.idiomatic: A boat is perilous, the bridge is safe, and a sage king does not court danger.

sentences.zh: 御史大夫言可聽。」

translations.literal: The commandant of the imperial counselors' words may be heeded."
translations.idiomatic: The commandant of the imperial counselors speaks with good sense."

sentences.zh: 上曰：「曉人不當如是邪！」

translations.literal: The sovereign said: "To enlighten a man ought it not be thus!"
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said, "Is this not the way to set a man right!"

sentences.zh: 乃從橋。

translations.literal: Thereupon he went by the bridge.
translations.idiomatic: He took the bridge.

sentences.zh: 後月餘，以歲惡民流，與丞相定國、大司馬車騎將軍史高俱乞骸骨，皆賜安車駟馬、黃金六十斤，罷。

translations.literal: After more than a month, because the year was bad and people drifted, together with Chancellor Dingguo and Grand Marshal General of Chariots and Cavalry Shi Gao all begged bones; all were bestowed comfortable carriage four-horse, sixty jin of gold, dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: A month later, citing a bad harvest and wandering refugees, he joined Chancellor Yu Dingguo and Grand Marshal Shi Gao in asking to retire; each received a comfortable carriage, four horses, sixty jin of gold, and was dismissed.

sentences.zh: 廣德為御史大夫，凡十月免。

translations.literal: Guangde as commandant of justice, in all ten months was dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: Guangde had held the post of commandant of the imperial counselors only ten months when he stepped down.

sentences.zh: 東歸沛，太守迎之界上。

translations.literal: East he returned to Pei; the grand administrator welcomed him at the border.
translations.idiomatic: He went home east to Pei, where the grand administrator met him at the commandery border.

sentences.zh: 沛以為榮，縣其安車傳子孫。

translations.literal: Pei took it as glory, suspended his comfortable carriage to transmit to sons and grandsons.
translations.idiomatic: Pei took pride in him and hung up the imperial carriage in the hall to hand down to his descendants.

sentences.zh: 平當字子思，祖父以訾百萬，自下邑徙平陵。

translations.literal: Ping Dang, courtesy name Zisi; his grandfather by assets of a million cash, from Xiayi moved to Pingling.
translations.idiomatic: Ping Dang, courtesy name Zisi, was descended from a grandfather worth a million cash who had moved the family from Xiayi to Pingling.

sentences.zh: 當少為大行治禮丞，功次補大鴻臚文學，察廉為順陽長、栒邑令，以明經為博士，公卿薦當論議通明，給事中。

translations.literal: Dang in youth was assistant for rites of the grand courier, by merit sequence filled vacancy as erudite of the grand herald, examined incorrupt became chief of Shunyang and magistrate of Xunyi, by clarity in the classics became erudite; excellencies recommended Dang's discussions as penetratingly clear, gave him palace attendant status.
translations.idiomatic: In youth he served as assistant for rites under the grand courier, rose through merit to an erudite post at the office of the grand herald, was nominated incorrupt and became chief of Shunyang and magistrate of Xunyi, then, on his mastery of the classics, was named an erudite; the high ministers praised the clarity of his memorials and he received appointment as palace attendant.

sentences.zh: 每有災異，當輒傅經術，言得失。

translations.literal: Whenever there were disasters and prodigies, Dang always applied classical learning, spoke of gain and loss.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever omens appeared he framed his analysis in the classics and spoke plainly of policy failures and successes.

sentences.zh: 文雅雖不能及蕭望之、匡衡，然指意略同。

translations.literal: In cultured elegance though he could not reach Xiao Wangzhi and Kuang Heng, yet his pointed meaning was broadly the same.
translations.idiomatic: His literary polish fell short of Xiao Wangzhi's or Kuang Heng's, but his aims ran along the same lines.

sentences.zh: 自元帝時，韋玄成為丞相，奏罷太上皇寢廟園，當上書言：「臣聞孔子曰：『如有王者，必世而後仁。』

translations.literal: From Emperor Yuan's time, Wei Xuancheng was chancellor, memorialized to abolish the Exalted Supreme Emperor's resting temple park; Dang submitted memorial saying: "Your servant has heard Confucius say: 'If there were a true king, after a generation then benevolence.'
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Yuan, Chancellor Wei Xuancheng had persuaded the throne to close the shrine park of the Exalted Supreme Emperor. Ping Dang memorialized: "Confucius said, 'Give a true king one generation and benevolence will appear.'"

sentences.zh: 三十年之間，道德和洽，制禮興樂，災害不生，禍亂不作。

translations.literal: Within thirty years, virtue and the Way harmonized and joined; regulated ritual and raised music; disasters did not arise, calamities and disorders did not occur.
translations.idiomatic: Within thirty years virtue and custom would blend, rites and music would flourish, and neither disaster nor rebellion would arise.

sentences.zh: 今聖漢受命而王，繼體承業二百餘年，孜孜不怠，政令清矣。

translations.literal: Now sagely Han received the mandate and ruled, inheriting the body receiving the enterprise over two hundred years, diligent without slackening; government orders are clear.
translations.idiomatic: Sacred Han has held the mandate for more than two hundred tireless years, and the statutes run clear.

sentences.zh: 然風俗未和，陰陽未調，災害數見，意者大本有不立與？

translations.literal: Yet customs are not harmonious, yin and yang are not tuned, disasters and prodigies repeatedly appear—I suspect perhaps the great root has something not established?
translations.idiomatic: Yet custom is still discordant, yin and yang are out of tune, and omens come thick and fast—can the great foundations still be unsettled?

sentences.zh: 何德化休征不應之久也！

translations.literal: How long virtuous transformation and auspicious signs have not responded!
translations.idiomatic: How long has virtuous rule waited in vain for its auspicious answer!

sentences.zh: 禍福不虛，必有因而至者焉。

translations.literal: Fortune and misfortune are not empty; there must be that by which they arrive.
translations.idiomatic: Blessing and calamity do not strike at random; each has its cause.

sentences.zh: 宜深跡其道而務修其本。

translations.literal: It is fitting deeply to trace its way and strive to cultivate its root.
translations.idiomatic: We should trace the way to its source and mend the root.

sentences.zh: 昔者帝堯南面而治，先『克膽俊德，以親九族』，而化及萬國《孝經》曰『天地之性人為貴，人之行莫大於孝，孝莫大於嚴父，嚴父莫大於配天，則周公其人也。』

translations.literal: Formerly Emperor Yao faced south and governed; first he was able to illumine matchless virtue and draw near the nine kindreds, and transformation reached the myriad states. The Classic of Filial Piety says: of heaven and earth's nature man is noblest; of man's conduct none is greater than filial piety; of filial piety none is greater than honoring the father; of honoring the father none is greater than matching heaven—then the Duke of Zhou was such a man.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Yao faced his court and began by cultivating matchless virtue and bringing harmony to his nine clans, until his influence spread to every state. The Classic of Filial Piety says that among heaven and earth's gifts none ranks above man, that among human deeds none ranks above filial piety, that honoring the father is filial piety's height, and that joining the father to heaven is its summit—and the Duke of Zhou is the model. The manuscript uses an uncommon graph where standard editions read "illume" in the line on Yao's virtue.

sentences.zh: 夫孝子善述人之志，周公既成文、武之業而製作禮樂，修嚴父配天之事，知文王不欲以子臨父，故推而序之，上極於後稷而以配天。

translations.literal: As for the filial son who well transmits others' aims, the Duke of Zhou when he had completed Wen and Wu's enterprise then made ritual and music, cultivated the affair of honoring the father matching heaven; knowing King Wen did not wish the son to stand over the father, therefore he pushed and ordered it, upward reaching to Hou Ji and thereby matching heaven.
translations.idiomatic: A true son carries on his forebears' purpose. The Duke of Zhou, having finished the work of Kings Wen and Wu, composed the rites and music and set his father King Wen beside heaven; knowing that a living son must not overshadow his father, he pushed the line back through the ancestors to Hou Ji so that Hou Ji might share heaven's honor.

sentences.zh: 此聖人之德，亡以加於孝也。

translations.literal: This is the sage's virtue; there is nothing whereby to add to filial piety.
translations.idiomatic: That is sage virtue, and filial piety cannot rise higher.

sentences.zh: 高皇帝聖德受命，有天下，尊太上皇，猶周文、武之追王太王、王季也。

translations.literal: The High Emperor by sagely virtue received the mandate, possessed the realm, honored the Exalted Supreme Emperor, just as Zhou Wen and Wu posthumously titled King Tai and Wang Ji.
translations.idiomatic: The High Emperor received heaven's mandate and ruled the world; he honored his father as Exalted Supreme Emperor just as Kings Wen and Wu of Zhou posthumously ennobled King Tai and Wang Ji.

sentences.zh: 此漢之始祖，後嗣所宜尊奉以廣盛德，孝之至也。

translations.literal: This is Han's first ancestor; posterity ought to honor and serve him so as to broaden splendid virtue—it is the utmost of filial piety.
translations.idiomatic: He is Han's founding forebear; later generations owe him reverent service to magnify the dynasty's virtue—that is filial piety in full measure.

sentences.zh: 《書》云：『正稽古建功立事，可以永年，傳於亡窮。』」

translations.literal: The Documents says: 'Correctly examine antiquity, establish merit and accomplish affairs—then you may prolong the years, transmit to without end.'"
translations.idiomatic: The Documents says, 'He who founds lasting deeds on the lessons of antiquity may enjoy long life and hand his work down forever.'"

sentences.zh: 上納其言，下詔復太上皇寢廟園。

translations.literal: The sovereign accepted his words, issued edict restoring the Exalted Supreme Emperor's resting temple park.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor accepted the argument and ordered the shrine park of the Exalted Supreme Emperor restored.

sentences.zh: 頃之，使行流民幽州。

translations.literal: Before long, he was sent to conduct drifting refugees in Youzhou.
translations.idiomatic: Soon afterward he was dispatched to Youzhou to oversee relief for displaced people.

sentences.zh: 舉奏刺史二千石勞徠有意者，言勃海鹽池可且勿禁，以救民急。

translations.literal: He reported up governors and full-two-thousand-shi who consoled and summoned with intent; said Bohai salt pools could temporarily not be forbidden, to relieve the people's urgency.
translations.idiomatic: He cited governors and two-thousand-shi officials who had worked earnestly to settle refugees and urged that the Bohai salt ponds be opened temporarily to ease the people's distress.

sentences.zh: 所過見稱，奉使者十一人，為最，遷丞相司直。

translations.literal: Where he passed he was praised; of eleven who bore mission he was foremost, promoted director of integrity for the chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: Wherever he went he won praise; among eleven commissioners on the same errand he ranked first and was promoted director of integrity for the chancellor.

sentences.zh: 坐法，左遷逆方刺史，復征入為太中大夫給事中，累遷長信少府、大鴻臚、光祿勳。

translations.literal: He sat for an offense under the law, was demoted governor of Nifang; again summoned in as grand counselor of the palace with palace attendant status, cumulatively promoted superintendent of the Changle Palace, grand herald, superintendent of the imperial household.
translations.idiomatic: A legal fault demoted him to governor of a northern frontier commandery whose name is miscopied in the received text; editors usually restore the name Shuofang. He was recalled as grand counselor of the palace with palace attendant duties and rose through superintendent of the Changle Palace, grand herald, and superintendent of the imperial household.

sentences.zh: 先是，太后姊子衛尉淳于長白言昌陵不可成，下有司議。

translations.literal: Earlier, the Empress Dowager's elder sister's son Commandant of the Guards Chunyu Chang had reported that Changling could not be completed; it was referred to the relevant offices for discussion.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier the Empress Dowager's nephew, Commandant of the Guards Chunyu Chang, had warned that the Changling project could not succeed, and the matter was sent to the ministries for debate.

sentences.zh: 當以為作治連年，可遂就。

translations.literal: Dang thought construction had run year after year and could be finished to completion.
translations.idiomatic: Ping Dang had argued that work already stretched over many years and could be brought to completion.

sentences.zh: 上既罷昌陵，以長首建忠策，復下公卿議封長。

translations.literal: After the sovereign had stopped Changling, because Chang first proposed the loyal plan, again ordered excellencies to discuss enfeoffing Chang.
translations.idiomatic: When the emperor abandoned Changling, he credited Chunyu Chang with the loyal warning and asked the high ministers whether to enfeoff him.

sentences.zh: 當又以為長雖有善言，不應封爵之科。

translations.literal: Dang again thought that although Chang had good words, he did not match the category for enfeoffment of rank.
translations.idiomatic: Ping Dang replied that Chang's counsel, however sound, did not meet the criteria for a noble title.

sentences.zh: 坐前議不正，左遷鉅鹿太守。

translations.literal: For sitting on the earlier discussion as not correct, he was demoted grand administrator of Julu.
translations.idiomatic: He was faulted for that earlier opinion and demoted to grand administrator of Julu.

sentences.zh: 後上遂封上。

translations.literal: Later the sovereign thereupon enfeoffed him.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor later enfeoffed Chunyu Chang anyway. The sentence is garbled in the manuscript; sense requires enfeoffing Chunyu Chang.

sentences.zh: 當以經明《禹貢》，使行河，為騎都尉，領河堤。

translations.literal: Dang by classics clarity in the Tribute of Yu was sent to conduct the Yellow River, became Commandant of Cavalry, supervised river dikes.
translations.idiomatic: For his mastery of the Tribute of Yu he was sent to inspect the Yellow River, appointed Commandant of Cavalry, and put in charge of the dikes.

sentences.zh: 哀帝即位，征當為光祿大夫、諸吏、散騎，復為光祿勳、御史大夫，至丞相。

translations.literal: When Emperor Ai ascended, Dang was summoned as Imperial Household Grandee, various clerks, attendant at leisure; again became superintendent of the imperial household, commandant of the imperial counselors, reaching chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Ai summoned him as Imperial Household Grandee with attendant-at-leisure privileges; he rose again to superintendent of the imperial household and commandant of the imperial counselors, and finally to chancellor.

sentences.zh: 以冬月，賜爵關內侯。

translations.literal: In a winter month, he was bestowed rank marquis within the passes.
translations.idiomatic: That winter he was given the rank of marquis within the passes.

sentences.zh: 明年春，上使使者召，欲封當。

translations.literal: The next spring, the sovereign sent envoys to summon, wishing to enfeoff Dang.
translations.idiomatic: The next spring the emperor sent messengers to call him to court for enfeoffment.

sentences.zh: 當病篤，不應召。

translations.literal: Dang's illness was grave; he did not respond to the summons.
translations.idiomatic: He was too ill to answer the summons.

sentences.zh: 室家或謂當：「不可強起受侯印為子孫耶？」

translations.literal: Family members someone said to Dang: "Could you not force yourself to rise and receive the marquis seal for sons and grandsons?"
translations.idiomatic: Someone in his household urged him, "Why not drag yourself to court, take the seal of a full marquis, and secure something for your heirs?"

sentences.zh: 當曰：「吾居大位，已負素餐之責矣，起受侯印，還臥而死，死有餘罪。

translations.literal: Dang said: "I have dwelt in a great post, already bear the blame of eating salary for nothing; to rise and receive the marquis seal, return and lie down and die—death would still leave surplus guilt.
translations.idiomatic: Dang answered, "I have held high office long enough to be ashamed of an empty salary; to rise now, clutch a marquis's seal, and die the moment I return to bed would leave guilt enough for the grave.

sentences.zh: 今不起者，所以為子孫也。」

translations.literal: Now not rising—this is the means whereby to act for sons and grandsons."
translations.idiomatic: My refusal to rise is precisely how I mean to help my descendants."

sentences.zh: 遂上書乞骸骨。

translations.literal: Thereupon he submitted memorial begging bones.
translations.idiomatic: He then memorialized for permission to retire.

sentences.zh: 上報曰：「朕選於眾，以君為相，視事日寡，輔政未久，陰陽不調，冬無大雪，旱氣為災，朕之不德，何必君罪？

translations.literal: The sovereign replied saying: "We chose from the multitude and made you chancellor; days in office are few, assisting government not long; yin and yang are not tuned, winter had no great snow, drought vapor became disaster—Our lack of virtue, why must it be your crime?
translations.idiomatic: The emperor answered, "We picked you from many men for chancellor; you have not long held the seal, yet yin and yang are awry, no heavy snow fell this winter, and drought stalks the land. The fault is Our lack of virtue—why blame you?

sentences.zh: 君何疑而上書乞骸骨，歸關內侯爵邑？

translations.literal: Why do you doubt and submit memorial begging bones, returning marquis-within-the-passes rank fief?
translations.idiomatic: Why doubt yourself, resign your post, and give back the income of a marquis within the passes?

sentences.zh: 使尚書令譚賜君養牛一，上尊酒十石。

translations.literal: We order Secretary Director Tan to bestow on you one fattening ox, ten stone of finest ale.
translations.idiomatic: We send Secretary Director Tan with a fattening ox and ten stone of finest ale.

sentences.zh: 君其勉致醫藥以自持。」

translations.literal: You should strive to obtain medicine to support yourself."
translations.idiomatic: Take physic and nurse your strength."

sentences.zh: 後月餘，卒。

translations.literal: After more than a month, he died.
translations.idiomatic: He died a little over a month later.

sentences.zh: 子晏以明經歷位大司徒，封防鄉侯。

translations.literal: His son Yan by clarity in the classics passed through posts to grand minister of education, was enfeoffed Marquis of Fang township.
translations.idiomatic: His son Ping Yan, a classicist, rose to grand minister of education and was enfeoffed as Marquis of Fang township.

sentences.zh: 漢興，唯韋、平父子至宰相。

translations.literal: Since Han rose, only Wei and Ping father-and-son reached chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: Since the founding of Han only the Wei and Ping families had both father and son serve as chancellor.

sentences.zh: 宣罷數歲，諫大夫鮑宣數薦宣。

translations.literal: After Xuan was dismissed several years, remonstrance grandee Bao Xuan repeatedly recommended Xuan.
translations.idiomatic: Some years after Peng Xuan retired, Remonstrance Grandee Bao Xuan repeatedly recommended him to the throne.

sentences.zh: 會元壽元年正月朔日蝕，鮑宣復言，上乃召宣為光祿大夫，遷御史大夫，轉為大司空，封長平侯。

translations.literal: It happened in the first year of Yuanshou, first month new moon solar eclipse; Bao Xuan again spoke; the sovereign then summoned Xuan as Imperial Household Grandee, promoted commandant of the imperial counselors, transferred to grand minister of works, enfeoffed Marquis of Changping.
translations.idiomatic: At the solar eclipse on the new moon of the first month of Yuanshou 1, Bao Xuan spoke again; the emperor recalled Peng Xuan as Imperial Household Grandee, then promoted him to commandant of the imperial counselors, grand minister of works, and Marquis of Changping.

sentences.zh: 會哀帝崩，新都侯王莽為大司馬，秉政專權。

translations.literal: It happened Emperor Ai died; Marquis of Xindu Wang Mang became grand marshal, wielded government and monopolized power.
translations.idiomatic: When Emperor Ai died, Wang Mang of Xindu became grand marshal and seized sole power.

sentences.zh: 宣上書言：「三公鼎足承君，一足不任，則覆亂美實。

translations.literal: Xuan submitted memorial saying: "The three dukes like a tripod's feet support the lord; if one foot does not bear, then it overturns and confuses the fine fruit.
translations.idiomatic: Peng Xuan memorialized that the three dukes are like a tripod that props the throne; if one leg fails, the vessel overturns and the good wine is spilled.

sentences.zh: 臣資性淺薄，年齒老眊，數伏疾病，昏亂遺忘，願上大司空、長平侯印綬，乞骸骨歸鄉里，俟置溝壑。」

translations.literal: Your servant's endowment is shallow and thin, years and teeth old and dim, repeatedly submits to illness, muddled and forgetful; I wish to present the grand minister of works, Marquis of Changping seals and ribbons, beg bones to return to village and neighborhood, await placement in ditch and gully."
translations.idiomatic: Your servant is shallow by nature, old and dim of eye, often ill, confused and forgetful; I beg to return the seals of grand minister of works and Marquis of Changping and retire to my home to await death in the ditch.

sentences.zh: 莽白太后，策宣曰：「惟君視事日寡，功德未效，迫於老眊昏亂，非所以輔國家、綏海內也。

translations.literal: Mang reported to the Empress Dowager, ordered Xuan with document: "Only you, days in office are few, merit and virtue not yet effected, pressed by old dimness and confusion—it is not the means to aid the state and pacify within the seas.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Mang informed the Empress Dowager and issued a rescript: "You have held office briefly and shown little merit; age and infirmity unfit you to steady the state within the four seas.

sentences.zh: 使光祿勳豐冊詔君，其上大司空印綬，便就國。」

translations.literal: Order Superintendent of the Imperial Household Feng with document to summon you; present up the grand minister of works seals and ribbons, then go to your state."
translations.idiomatic: Let Superintendent of the Imperial Household Wang Feng bring the edict: surrender the seals of the grand minister of works and go at once to your fief.

sentences.zh: 莽恨宣求退，故不賜黃金、安車駟馬。

translations.literal: Mang resented Xuan seeking withdrawal, therefore did not bestow gold, comfortable carriage four-horse.
translations.idiomatic: Mang resented his asking to withdraw and withheld the usual gift of gold, carriage, and four horses.

sentences.zh: 宣居國數年，薨，謚曰頃侯。

translations.literal: Xuan dwelt in his state several years, died, posthumous title Marquis Qing.
translations.idiomatic: Peng Xuan lived on his fief a few years, died, and was posthumously titled Marquis Qing.

sentences.zh: 傳子至孫，王莽敗，乃絕。

translations.literal: Passed son reaching grandson; Wang Mang fell, then was cut off.
translations.idiomatic: The title passed to his son and grandson until Wang Mang fell and the line ended.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：雋不疑學以從政，臨事不惑，遂立名跡，終始可述。

translations.literal: The appraiser says: Jun Buyi by learning to follow government, facing affairs was not confused, thereby established name and traces; from start to finish can be narrated.
translations.idiomatic: The historian's judgment: Jun Buyi turned classical learning into firm government; he never wavered in a crisis and left a name worth telling from first to last.

sentences.zh: 疏廣行止足之計，免辱殆之累，亦其次也。

translations.literal: Shu Guang practiced the plan of knowing stopping and sufficiency, avoided the burden of disgrace and peril—also next in rank.
translations.idiomatic: Shu Guang knew when enough was enough and so escaped shame and danger—a worthy second.

sentences.zh: 於安國父子哀鰥哲獄，為任職臣。

translations.literal: Yu Dingguo father and son pitied widowers and widows, were wise in prison cases, were ministers fit for office.
translations.idiomatic: Yu Dingguo and his son showed mercy to the helpless and wisdom in judgment—true ministers for their posts.

sentences.zh: 薛廣德保縣車之榮，平當逡遁有恥，彭宣見險而止，異乎「苟患失之」者矣。

translations.literal: Xue Guangde preserved the glory of the suspended carriage; Ping Dang withdrew with a sense of shame; Peng Xuan saw peril and stopped—different from those who 'if only worried about losing.'
translations.idiomatic: Xue Guangde kept the honor of a carriage hung up for good; Ping Dang stepped back with a sense of shame; Peng Xuan turned back when he saw the cliff ahead—men unlike those who cling to office for fear of poverty.
